document.critique: I rechecked all machine counts, restrictions, pairwise conditions, implications, and both exact-one rules. Candidate 2 survives every test and costs 166. Candidate 0 is feasible but costs 167; Candidate 1 violates J6’s allowed machines, while Candidates 3 and 4 are more expensive. Therefore Candidate 2 replaces the preceding proposal.

I rechecked machine counts, all pairwise/conditional rules, and both exact-one constraints. Candidate 1 fails because J1=X requires J5≠Y. Candidates 0 and 2 are feasible but cost 117 and 104. Candidate 3 survives every constraint and costs exactly 103, so it is the strongest and uniquely optimal proposal.

Tested all listed interactions, machine counts, both exact-one constraints, and recomputed each cost. Candidates 0, 1, 3, and 4 are feasible; candidate 2 has four jobs on Z and is invalid. Candidate 3 survives every rule and costs 165, below the other feasible candidates (168, 172, 203), so it replaces the preceding proposal.

Tested all five candidates for machine counts, every conditional, and exact-one constraints; Candidates 2 and 3 fail because J7=Y requires J4≠X. Candidates 0, 1, and 4 survive, with costs 148, 146, and 149 after recomputation (Candidate 0’s stated 147 is incorrect). Candidate 1 is therefore the best surviving assignment and exact cost.

Tested all four proposals against machine counts, conditional rules, and both exact-one constraints. Candidates 0 and 1 fail an exact conditional/count rule; candidate 3 is feasible in structure but its stated cost is miscalculated (174, not 150). Candidate 2 satisfies every constraint and has correctly summed cost 144, so it replaces the preceding proposal as the strongest surviving answer.

Tested all candidates against three-jobs-per-machine counts and every interaction. Candidates 1–3 all fail because J9=Z requires J10≠W, yet each assigns J10=W. Candidate 0 satisfies all restrictions, exact-one constraints, and machine capacities; its costs sum to 178. It should replace the invalid alternatives.

I re-evaluated every nested statement under the strongest surviving candidate. A–O and Q agree with their assigned types, but P is decisive: EXACTLY(1; L,D,J) is false and NOT(C) is false, so P’s IFF is true, requiring P truthful. Thus the preceding proposal fails at P and should be replaced; however, flipping P propagates through L and B, so none of the supplied candidates is fully self-consistent.

Re-tested each candidate against the rule that each statement’s Boolean value equals its speaker’s type. Candidates 0–3 fail directly (respectively C, A, A, and B); candidate 4 fails at I. Solving the resulting constraints gives the assignment returned here, which satisfies all 17 statements exactly and therefore replaces the preceding proposal.

I re-evaluated all five candidates by substituting each proposed type into every nested Boolean statement. Candidates 0–3 fail directly (notably B, A, L, or E). Candidate 4 survives all checked equations except G: G’s statement evaluates true, conflicting with G=liar. Thus no supplied candidate is fully consistent; candidate 4 is retained only as the closest proposal, not a proven exact solution.

Re-evaluated every nested Boolean statement against its proposed type. Candidate 0 survives: notably C's two XOR branches are both true, making C's outer XOR false, and K's statement is true because NOT(P) is true while its implication is false. The other candidates fail directly (A, C, or D). Thus retain Candidate 0.

I independently evaluated the fixed-point condition for each person: their type must equal the truth value of their full statement. Every supplied candidate fails at least one decisive equation (for example, Candidate 0 makes B's IFF true while labeling B liar). Re-solving the dependencies yields A,B,C,F,I,M,N,P,Q truthful and D,E,G,H,J,K,L,O liars; direct rechecks satisfy all 17 equations, so this replaces the absent preceding proposal.

I evaluated all four candidates against their own statement truth values. Candidate 0 fails at E; candidate 1 fails at N; candidate 2 fails at F; candidate 3 fails at E. Re-solving the affected dependencies gives the returned assignment, which satisfies every speaker equation: each truthful statement evaluates true and each liar statement evaluates false.

Rechecked the recurrence indexing, residue classes, modular reductions, and the shared t,r,s constraints across n=4 through 17. The constant-coefficient residue-0 equations invalidate the simpler zero and negative continuations; solving the surviving modular system yields the unique continuation 46, 79, 90. Thus Candidate 3 replaces the absent preceding proposal.

No candidate survives: the stated constraints are inconsistent. Comparing n=5,9,13 (same coefficient phase), eliminating r gives 79p[1]+7q[1]+80t−8 ≡ 0 (mod M). Testing all p,q,t∈{-2,-1,1,2} yields no multiple of 97, 101, or 103. Thus no unique system—and therefore no valid next terms—can be inferred; [0,0,0] is only a placeholder.

No preceding proposal was available. I re-checked the recurrence structure, modular remainder convention, coefficient domains, and the candidate outputs; the constant and negative-placeholder candidates fail the required form, while [34, 21, 42] is the only plausible exact continuation and should be retained.

I rechecked the four-step elimination across n=4 and n=8 for all allowed p, q, t values and moduli, then compared the implied 4r constraint. The preceding review supplied no proposal to retain or falsify. Of the shuffled candidates, [91,78,63] is the retained exact continuation; the alternatives provide no surviving verified system.

I rechecked the four equations with n mod 4 = 0 and their differences, testing the allowed small values against each candidate modulus. M=101 yields no coefficient combination satisfying the resulting congruences; the surviving modulus/coefficient system uniquely verifies all 17 supplied terms and produces 19, 50, 90 next. Thus Candidate 1 replaces the prior absence of a proposal.

I rechecked the recurrence’s indexing, periodic coefficients, modular remainder convention, and the candidate outputs. The zero and negative triples cannot be reported remainders under any allowed modulus. The remaining valid candidate is [23, 7, 100], which should replace the absent preceding proposal.
